speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: How much did you get?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I just turned it on now.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, maybe that's interesting to you. Why don't you say that, if you want to talk to me, about this statement that I made about if we're not interested, I believe that if we're not interested in where we came from, we're not interested in where we're going. And then the reason that I started to write, or got the...

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: What would I say?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: That I became interested in writing the family story to tabulate the... get some knowledge of the family heritage. And then... So I went back to, as far as I could remember, and then the information I got from my mother led me to find interest in other sources of interest to the early history of Moscow.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Do you want to turn it off and start it again?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Maybe I could say it better.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh no, that's fine. Don't worry about...

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, that's the way I started. Then I had the opportunity to make three trips to Europe and to the Scandinavian country and visit the island where my father was born, where his ancestors, where our ancestors were born. I don't know how many of them. I think we came from Germany, really. Because I have no way of knowing how many of my ancestors came to... came from originally from Sweden. They may have come from Germany. And even France, because there were a lot of French people in the Scandinavian country. And I had heard my mother's talk about this place where she came from in western, in her parents, where her parents came from in western Sweden. Gothenburg, near Gothenburg, John Chipping, John Coping, the capital city of the... the capital city of... the matching industry. It was the biggest at one time. It had the biggest manufacturing... manufacturing place of all of Europe and the match industry. Well, then they came to America. I won't go into detail on that. I told that in a book.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: You have a book at home, do you?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And I told that, so maybe you read that or can read it there. And my going to Sweden is information you can get in the book.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: What made them want to come to Sweden? Do you know what made them want to come to the United States from Sweden?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, I know that. My father told that over and over again. Now, like my father was born on this island, of course he never met my mother until she came to America, to Moscow. And my father came from... landed in Philadelphia and came to Moscow 17... it was 1776 and came to Moscow in 1886. And my mother came with her parents from Minnesota to Moscow in 1887, one year apart. That might be vice versa, but that's close enough. And so my father was still excavating the railroad here and he stopped in this town and he met my mother. And she was only 13 years old, so he just waited till she grew up and at 17 they were married. And he was 10 years young or 12 years older.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: But you ask about why they came to America. During that time there had been plagues. For example, even the one play that my father mentioned that was handed down, handed down, handed down in family circles. From 1350, one of the biggest plagues they ever had, in fact most of the population was wiped out. And they... it was in Germany and it was in Sweden. And that's why they had Ombre -Amergau, because some of the people in Germany decided that they had... they had to rely on their creation of God, on their God for preservation. And they started this Ombre -Amergau, which every 10 years they would have this passion play in this little town in Germany. And they had started that several hundred years ago, I guess. And they still have it every 10 years. And there are just any number of people from America, from the world over, who congregate in that little town and I was there.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: But the year that I was there was not the year that they were playing this, see?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: It was in the between towns every 10 years. And they were mostly local people that were trained to take part in that. It was a very interesting little town, beautiful little place. I'll never forget when we came... the tour guide told us, close your eyes for a few minutes, close your eyes and fold your hands and just wait. And I'll tell you when to open them. And he said, when we opened our eyes, we were in the bus, and we looked out and it was just almost like a heavenly scene to see that little city below, that little town or village, German village below there. And all the beautiful, beautiful mountains and the lakes. I've got pictures of them, I wish I could show them to you. And I just... it was just gorgeous. And they took us to the theater place where they had this play every 10 years. And we saw the crown, the Christ war, and all the paraphernalia that they had for this play, including the Roman costumes and the chariots and the table where they had the Last Supper. And everything they needed for that passion play, and they had it in storage. And they allowed us, the tourists, 28 of us, to see all that in storage there. And we saw the people, melling along the town, many of them had beards, so they could look as much like the people in Christ time as possible, see. And they were trained to take their parts, and when they grew up others should take their parts, but mostly all local people. Well, we won't take too much time for that. But... and I wish that the world, and these young people are doing a lot of thinking, and I'm all for them. And I just feel like they're going to solve a lot of the problems of today just because they want to step back and see what they can do to make this world better. And I believe that. And of course we know that there are lots of hoodlums. There are hoodlums, and there are criminals by the thousand, but after all they're a minority. And I still think our world is going to get better. My brother even said, who knows, maybe Heaven's going to be right on this earth. After the period of destruction.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: That's when my brother, the one that passed away said, and he believed it, I think. Well, that as it may, we don't know what Heaven's like, but we know that this world is really going to find a new goal, and a new inspiration to do, to lead for all of us to enjoy a better life. And above all things, I think we have to eliminate waste. Like the oil situation, and that has just been terrific, and we just can't waste the resources like that.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Doesn't sound logical?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: You know, you're one of my, and I'm not put here for any special reason. I've always tried to lead a good life, but I haven't been a good church member or anything like that. And I worked hard, we worked hard on the farm, and real hard, and we were very close. Togetherness was one thing in our family that I can appreciate more as I've grown older and lived in retirement by myself. And now my brothers, I was scattered all over the United States, two of them are gone, but they just remember me with cards and calls, and they're doing a lot of good work in this world in their little way. Just like one, two, three of them are taking up drawing and painting. Well, they've all, our family's been kind of artistically minded. That doesn't mean we were professionals, any of us, but they are a number of them. They make a soul very well and are very artistic. My sister and boys, she just makes beautiful clothes, and her daughter is following in her footsteps. She graduated from home actually, and she just can make a dress in no time at all, in a beautiful dress. And I'm not bragging about what our family's done, because we've made just as many mistakes as other people, but I still think that as they grow older, they just realize what closeness there was, and how our parents taught us principles that are maybe pioneer principles of our state, our town, and our country. And I'm glad that I can remember as much as I do of the early history, and a lot of it was told, some of it was told, by my mother. When she was a child, she came here. She remembers the beginning of the university.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Could you tell that story about how the university came to be where it was?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You mentioned that your mother knew something about how it came to be. Well, she was here.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: See?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yes, the beginning of, the beginning of, first we'll go back to the beginning of Moscow, which was the out east of Moscow on what we call Mountain View Road now, was the first location, and Mr. Lou Allen, his brother I think, started a little store out there, and they took up a homestead in possibly 1870 or 71, and they built a little store out there, and had a mailbox in this store, just a box, just a shoe box, I think. Not a shoe box, I don't think they had shoe boxes. But they had a box, and all the mail that came by horseback was kept in that little grocery store, and all around the store, you know, on that farm. Well, then there were several, a great many homesteaders that came here to take up homestead, because the Homestead Acts had been enacted in Washington, and they were issuing homestead certificates to a great many people from the east, way back east. And they came by wagon, you know, covered wagons, and they came by horseback, before the railroads were started, and they came out west to take homesteads, and just came in flocks, just load them. And so there were a lot of homesteaders who started around Moscow, who started their new life around Moscow in the early 1870s, I'll say. There were some that came before them, a few that came before them, but not very many. Most of them came in the 1870s.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And so the government decided to put a post office in here, and they built the little first post office, you saw that building, I showed you that post office, didn't I?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: They built a log house, and put it down there by this store, by Llewelyn store. And this log house was the first post office, and they named it Paradise City.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And most of the mail came from Lewiston, before the Lewiston -Clarke people, I'd even know. No, it wasn't before Lewiston -Clarke. But they came from Lewiston, because Lewiston -Clarke, having come to Lewiston, they named Lewiston and Clarke, you know. And so most of the mail came by horseback up here to Moscow's post office. But so many of the homesteaders later, just a little bit later in the 1972 and 1973, lived farther west and south of our present Moscow. So they decided that Paradise City wasn't the most convenient place, and that they should move the post office and the town, a new town, to what is now Main Street of Moscow. And that Main Street of Moscow was a logging trail that the homesteaders had used to call logs from the Moscow Mountains, where a Mr. Barton, R .H. Barton, had a sawmill, had built a new sawmill. And they hauled the logs for their homestead houses, for their homestead log houses, south of Moscow. And personally, I remember five of those log houses. And I'm the only one, being the oldest one. I'm the oldest one in the family who remembers that. And there was the Delmon Homestead. John and Newton Delmon were two brothers that had taken up the farm that my folks purchased, or my father, because he purchased it before he was married. And that was the Delmon Homestead. Then there was the North of Homestead, just west of that. And west of our farm, our old farm, which is south of Moscow, about two and a quarter miles, southeast, a little bit east, of the Foothills, or near the Paradise Ridge. And of course, Paradise Ridge, whether they named Paradise Post Office after the Ridge, or if the Ridge acquired its name from that post office, it's more likely that the Ridge acquired the name from the post office. And over this Paradise Ridge, there had been a trail, Indian Trail, going down to the river, which was down, a steep trail, down to the river at the present Lewiston and Clarkston. And that was the trail that was used. And that came over the Paradise Mountains, down to Paradise, and eventually to Moscow. And Governor Stevenson of the Northwest Territory, which comprised Oregon, Washington, and the state of Idaho, was, let's see now, we'll say, that was one territory called the Northwest Territory. And this Governor Stevenson, he heard about the rich land in the Palouse Hills, in the present Palouse Country. And so he and 29 men came horseback from this Washington Territory. I don't remember the name of the place he came from. Very likely, oh, no, I had one wall of wall, I don't think. It could have been Walla Walla. And he came through this territory, and he was surprised to see the wonderful growth of grass, and the lush, lush growth of everything in this Palouse Country. And where it became, or why it was named the Palouse Country, was by the Indians, who had lived here long before, you know, ages before. And they had raised horses, their own horses. And they called them the Appaloosa horses. And that means Appaloosa, from Appaloosa, see, Appaloosa, Appaloosa. I read that in the literature that the Appaloosa headquarters, which is in Moscow, United States headquarters of the Appaloosa horses, is in Moscow. And, you know, that and so. And so they called, they had, they raised Appaloosa horses, known for their very kind breed and very stable horses. And that's the beginning of the Appaloosa industry. Originally, the Appaloosas were supposed to have come from Spain and to California. But I won't go into that. And now, going into the history of Moscow, they moved that city or paradise post office to Main Street in Moscow. They didn't have a post office. They had it in the building, one of their early buildings. It was really on Main Street. And Mr. Llewellyn bought, let's see, I like, he didn't buy land there that they put the post office on. They put the post office on this log road, which became Main Street in Moscow. And the town of Moscow was named, I accepted, and I told that, accepted what one man from Pennsylvania wanted to not name Moscow, Paradise City, but Moscow, because he'd come from a town in Moscow. Pennsylvania. And he thought that'd be, and his son lived here, and his son had, had told some of the early pioneers of Moscow this story. And so that will accept that as the, the reason that Moscow was named Moscow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who was it who named the town Moscow? What was the name?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, his name, I had that name, but I...

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Now, was that Neff? Was it Neff who?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Mr.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Neff? Neff? Was it Neff?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Wait a little, I'll tell you. Before you go, we'll look that up, because it's in here. It's in here. And we'll look that up, because his story is in there. And he told me this. He was in my home. I entertained Mr. David in my home, and we discussed some of those early things. Because I was born in 1890, and that's the year that he came here. But he was 10 or 11 years old when he came here from Wisconsin with his parents, the Davids family. And I'll, we'll look up that and verify that, but I think that, I think Neff had a homestead out there, too. And I, and, but I'm not really sure about that. But some of the first people that lived on Main Street was the, uh, Blue Allens. There were two, I think two, two Blue Allens brothers that started the grocery store. And then there was Dr. Neffia. Dr.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, maybe not the, maybe not the first. The grocery store was one of the first. But when my mother came here in 1886, or seven, 87 maybe, why, she said there was, um, uh, the grocery store, the Blue Allens grocery store, and there was, um, Barton's, they, they built the prerunner, or the first little boarding house, and that was the McGregor house. McGregor, not McGregor, but Barton house, which, later, which burned the year that my mother came here in 1887, presumably. And, um, and then that old wooden structure burnt down, and it was a few years later where they built the Moscow hotel, the Preston Moscow Hotel. And, uh, mother remembers when, uh, I told that story in the, in the book about how they had,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: building burns, they were so concerned about, about these gamblers, and, and the, with the gambling there were always, uh, women who, who lived in a, what would I say, a, a red light district, in a home of their own. And I remember that home, and I can show you where that home is right now. It's down there by the old, um, northern part of Moscow, where the, near, where the great northern depot was. And that house remained here, but of course they were, that, that, uh, was outlawed in Moscow.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Gambling and, and, uh, all that went with it was outlawed in Moscow years and years ago, even before I remember.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The gambling places were right behind the, uh, the Barton house? Yeah? Yeah? Yeah?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Great, they, right where the new addition to the hotel, see that addition was built, uh, on, uh, onto the mosque, Mr. Wright owned it. And he was the owner when I, went there in 1927. And he had, uh, he built a new addition to the hotel, built 40 new rooms. But that place where the new addition was, on the west end of Moscow Hotel, why, uh, west addition was, um, used to be the old gambling joint. And all, they, all, there were miners, there were miners. There was Golden Idaho, you know, way up in the Florence, uh, district, and Dixie, and all around there, even Grangeville, and Orofino. Orofino was one of the biggest, well, I could just talk all day and tell some of that. And, um, Orofino, and Orofino means fine ore. Fine ore was the, was gold, of course. And that's how Orofino was named, Orofino. From, from a group of men that came up to Moscow to see if they could find gold. Well, they didn't find gold here. Now that was, um, I often know who that group was, but I can't say it right now. And that, um, because I've read a lot of history about Moscow, and, uh, about this territory of Idaho, North Idaho. And we'll, we'll have to get to the university. And, um, they, uh, these men didn't find gold around Moscow, and they went horseback down the Indian Trail, and went up the Clearwater River, and looked for gold. And they, a few of them found some traces of gold in the Clearwater River. So they stayed overnight there, or camp there near Orofino. And, uh, and they found more gold. And so they went, finally, they went up to, um, what is now Pierce, or Greer, Pierce, I think, and discovered rich finds of gold. And that, that was the beginning of, uh, or not, maybe the beginning, because gold had been mined down in Haley, and, and all over in Idaho. There had been many, many mining concerns in Idaho. It became, that's why it's called Jam State, because there was so much gold, and so many gems, you know, rubies and everything in Idaho. And it was named the Jam State when it became a state. But they had belonged to the Northwest Territory, and, and, uh, Lewis and Clark came here to this territory, to the Washington, Oregon, maybe in the 18, I think 1826, or in that, about that time. And they hadn't done very much to that Northwest Territory. There were some people, some, uh, people came up from California and that, and settled along the coast. But the, the, the state of Washington, which was part of the territory, they big, the lovely, uh, a lot of homesteaders came here to settle in this part of the country. And they were all made up of homesteads. And as I say, I remember five of those homestead cabins. And then there were many, many more that I didn't know about, that I don't remember, that were gone before I remember.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Are there other things that your mother told you about, you know, things that were in Moscow that she remembers?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, she said that she remembered this doctor, uh, I don't know, uh, who was up there. And now I can't say it. There was a dentist, Macaulay, was the first dentist in Moscow, and he was on Main Street because she had to go to him. When she first came with her parents from Minnesota, she was, her mother took her to this dentist, Macaulay. And that was Margaret Macaulay's father. Margaret Macaulay was one of the earliest graduates of the University of Idaho, and a personal friend of mine. But of course she was a lot older than I was. She graduated, and I think she graduated in 1901, the same year Homer David did. And, um, and then my cousin Adrian Nelson graduated in the second class of the University in 1897. There was one more class before that in 1886, and I think there were only four, either four or six graduates in that first class. But now my mother remembered the beginning. She was a girl here, and she did babysitting for the, um, what, what, the whole, not ride -and -ball, but, um,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: bigum, not bigum, what was the name of that? Sweet?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Sweet. Sweet. The sweet, she, the, uh, uh, a net, um, a niece of Mr. Sweet was Mrs. Hannah, and she did babysitting. And she was a piano teacher, and her home was on the corner of the street, which is where the, the city library now is. And that, I remember that little home, where she worked. She showed me that. And that became the library, the Carnegie Library, they took that, they moved that home, or tore it down, and, um, it was the home of Willa, of Sweet, of Hannah. Hannah, and Mr. Sweet was the, was the niece of Belle Sweet, I think, and she was her cousin, maybe, on Belle Sweet. And, um, Mr. Sweet, who, who, whom the Sweet Hall is named from, named from, was one of the originators of the University of Idaho. There were people who were interested in establishing the University of Idaho at Moscow, who were Willy Sweet and McConnell, J .W. W. McConnell, who was the, for one of the first earliest governors of the territory of Idaho, and who also is, um, built this, his mansion here, called all the timber, or all the material, from Walla Walla, Washington, to build his mansion, which is now the museum. That's where my mother told her. And my mother visited that house, which was completed by the time they came to Moscow in 1887. I think that town, because she had the privilege of visiting this home, or going through this lovely new home.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And, and, and that, I, I think I'm correct in saying it maybe was 1887, right?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: That it may have been 1888 before, before she got to see this home. I don't know that. And then there, another person who was interested in was Mr. Brigham. He lived, had a homestead out by a genesee, had taken up homestead, I believe I'm right about that. And, and he, and then Willy Sweet and Governor McConnell, and a man from the very first editors, or owners, of the Spokesman Review, came down to Moscow because they were urged by some of these people like the, um, um, Dr. McCauley, and the new, another doctor had come here because there had been so many deaths, so many children died from death theory, I think it was, that they were, they were concerned about getting a doctor here, and they got this doctor from, from, um, Colfax. And now, I can't say that name, Dr. Dr. Well, I'll think of it in a short time, but that doctor was the first doctor and he had his home here.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And then, is that Dr. Blake?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yes, Dr. Blake is right. Dr. Blake. And Dr. Blake was the mother, father of Mrs. Summerfield, that Summerfield was a sheriff here, and Mrs. Summerfield died out at the county home a few years ago, and her son,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: her son still lives in Moscow. He used to, he used to work with the Wonder Bread all the years that I was at the hotel, and I knew him all the time, I used to say, well, just think, your mother and my mother were friends. And, um, and, um, and, um, and, uh, some of the earliest pioneers of Moscow. And that's right, Dr. Blake, see, I told you that the young people are the ones that can think, and my thinking is, is waning. But, um, and then there was a white drugstore, and white was Frank White, who had that drugstore, and he also had a homestead out here where East Moscow is. And that's why they named White Avenue after him. But why, what bothers me is that when I was a child, we called that Scatter Bowl Lane, or Scatter Bowl Road, Scatter Bowl Road, I think we called it. The farm is now where there's warehouse, the grocery warehouse, and their automobile companies. And the home, the old Scatter Bowl home, was on the other side of the railroad track, but I'm going to say that there was no county road or state highway. Running parallel to the railroad until possibly some 20 or 25, maybe 25 years ago. I'm just guessing of that. And we went to the, when we came from Moscow, we'd go out, pair, White Avenue, or let me call it Scatter Bowl Avenue, Scatter Bowl Road, and turn at the corner there on what is now Mountain View Road, and go past the west side of the cemetery where we turn onto the Troy Road, which runs parallel from there, from that southwest corner of the cemetery. It runs parallel to the Northern Pacific Railroad, which my dad did all the excavating for, all through Moscow, all the way from Spokane, down to Juliaetta, to Troy, and down the canyon to Juliaetta. The reason that he had to stop his contract ended at Juliaetta was because the Indian Reservation, Nez Perce Reservation, had just been created by the Congress, or by the, in Washington, and they couldn't go into that Nez Perce Reservation with the railroad. Until years, several, a number of years later, when they built the railroad into Lewiston. And their passengers, the passengers from Lewiston, were taken by team, usually not to Juliaetta, because that was a crooked and long railroad down that canyon, and steep canyon, you know what it's like. And they took them to Genesee, and for a number of years, until they built that railroad into Lewiston, and they had to come from Lewiston up to Genesee, if they wanted to go to Spokane or someplace like that. Now let's see. Now about the university, beginning of the university. My mother, they summoned a man, there was old MJ Shields, who owned the first electric plant in a lumber mill, down in the center of Moscow, right now, east of Moscow's main street, about where, just due west of what the hospital, where the hospital is now. And that old hospital, the old hospital, was remodeled into the first, was remodeled from the old McGregor house. Which was a boarding house that the homesteader, McGregor, had built as a boarding house. So there was McGregor house, and Barton house, and then there was later, and very early, we had, we called it, Del North Hotel, which is where Moscow Manor now is. And that was a very ritchie hotel for a small town.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And then they had a - What was that like when it was a - The biggest just now.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: The Del North Hotel, they had a very, very nice town now. Homer Davis remembers that, but that was because I didn't get to go to hotels or go to town very much. You know, to anything, but he was going, he was ten years older than I was, and he went, was one of the first students among, not one of the first students, but in the class of nineteen -one, see. So he went to the university, and he can remember about how he walked, walked up to university, and if they wanted to have a date, and they'd take their dates down to this Del North Hotel and down to the city.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: What did they call that? Civic Center or something?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: It was the beginning of the first chamber of commerce, really. But they had a building, what is now the McCoy apartment house. And that was a very, very swanky, for those days, a very swanky, a hosslery, hosslery, I guess you'd call it, where they, or center, where they entertained, they had, oh, had gambling or drinking, and that, a bar, because that, it wasn't dry until the prohibition days, you know. I remember all about that.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And, and they had, he told me.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever eat out in town when you went to town?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: No, never. The first, you asked me if I ate out in town. Of course, we didn't, our big family, we didn't eat out. Not very many other people did. And that's why, not very many people who came from the little towns around, like Troy and, and, um, Bo -ville and, and, um, well, Bo -ville was started later, a lot later. But, Genesee and all those places, they, they never, those farmers and homesteaders, they never thought about staying at a hotel or even at the boarding house, like the Frye. The Frye Hotel was owned by the Carters, the old Carters, and that was, it's still there, the old Carter boarding house. And, and that's on the corner of Jackson and Third, you know, the Frye, Frye, well, it's in the parking house now. And, um, but we didn't, it wasn't, it was very unusual for people to eat at hotels, you know, and all the hotels, including the Moscow Hotel at first, I didn't remember, but my mother did and my father, that they had, um, uh, uh, family -style serving of food. They just, see, they didn't, they didn't have individual tables, they just had tables, long tables, and they served as family -style. And, uh, whoever came in, they stacked with these other people, that's the way they got a quin. That was the way they, uh, conversed. And then they had the benches outside, on the, outside the hotel, there was always a bench for the people that were all the old men and the old, um, um, boys who, who, uh, didn't have a job in the summertime, would sit on those benches and converse, and watch, uh, the traffic go by on the dirt road, the dust in the summer, was inches thick, and in the winter there was mud, deep mud, oh, just like very thick soup. And I can remember when, uh, seeing the sticks, uh, the rails, old rails, they stick them in the sinkholes, so that the, the people coming by in the, in wagons wouldn't, their horses wouldn't step into those sinkholes and, and the, uh, ground, you might say, or stucks, and they couldn't get them out. And I remember that now, and I remember, I was even, had started college before they paved Main Street and Washington Street, first Main Street and then Washington Street, and I, and that was 1911, that they paved, paved those streets.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: We thought we were getting, or is it now living in a city at that time?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And, um...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What were the boardwalks like? The boardwalks?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh, they were just board, and they were, uh, maybe made out of, uh, two by, uh, oh, by two, two by twos, or two by four, small, maybe, kind of wide boards, more like planks, you know, on those Main Street, they had heavier sidewalks. And then, of course, they had to have sidewalks on all the streets in town later, later, because they just had, like any little village, why they didn't have sidewalks at first, but then later they were required, had picket fences along the, along the houses and, uh, and the board sidewalks, and they were narrower in the town, but they were wider in, um, in the, on Main Street.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they off the ground, or did they...

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh, yeah, they, they had to make them higher than the, than the ground level, because we had a lot of rain, a lot of mud, a lot of dust, and, uh, so it was feasible to make the sidewalks higher. Not only that, there was lots of rain here, lots of water pools, down by the river tracks on Southern Main Street, what is now Southern Main Street, where there were deep, uh, ditch, uh, runs, or creeks, or, or water bodies under the sidewalks, and we, they had high sidewalks, they were raced up, maybe five feet, five or six feet, at least five or six feet, and I remember when we went to school, um, we always, we lived three quarters of a mile south of that crosswalk, south of, it was really an extension of Mountain View Road south, three quarters of a mile from the railroad, and we walked that three quarters of a mile to school, and I'm in walk, I'll say that, I'm walk most of the time, the weather was real stormy, but read my books, because I've described that in detail, read my book today, it's our Bichon rate, and, and you'll learn about how we walked to school, and how we walked when we were four years old, to town, with our folks many and many times, because they didn't, it was easier for my father and my mother in those days when they were young, to walk to Moscow, then to go out and hitch up the team, and take a lumber wagon, or a hack, and go, and go to town, but on certain occasions, when the weather was bad, the snow was deep, why we were taken to school by our dad, and we thought that was a rare treat when it happened to be snow, and we could ride in the, in the sleigh to school, and we were fortunate in having a cutter, and I mean a cutter, a double seat cutter, with a post, a poster in broadcloth, dark green broadcloth, had a nice dashboard, we had a diving team, all with the diving team, nice driving team, which, that was kept going very, very well, and sleigh bells when it was snow, and that was a treat to get to go to school and have other kids envious, because we got to ride in a cutter to school. But we also rode in the hack, in the mud, when the mud, when, clear up to the bottom of the hack, in the spring, and no sand, no paving, nothing but mud, mud, mud, and Moscow's know that they said, they bet that there wasn't, somebody made the remark, they bet there wasn't one pair of clean shoes in Moscow during the winter, early winter. And that's about right. Well, I still haven't finished my story about the, about the University and my mother serving, serving these men who were called together, because they had been, and there was a, even in that early day, in Southern Idaho wasn't anything, there was no irrigation. I remember when the first irrigation project in Southern Idaho took place, and I, I was quite a big girl, when that happened. And so they, Moscow was a logical place for a university, and the state of Washington territory became a state, and they decided to make their, locate their state's university, or state's school, Seattle, I guess, had been started earlier, the University of Washington had been started earlier. I don't know what year, at Seattle, but they were going to build another school, which they built just about the same time as we did, about 1890 was a year of our university. And, and they started their university, agricultural college, which is WSU Washington State University, Washington State University, it was at first state college, and then became Washington State University. But it was Washington State College when I went to school, and this country, there was no equal, either in the state of Washington, or in the state of Idaho, when it came to the soil content, and the forgetfulness of the pollute soil. So it was logical that they would make these two agricultural colleges in, in this pollute country. And, and to this day, maybe a lot of people don't appreciate it. But to this day, why we would say that it was fortunate for the city of Moscow, and the city of Pullman, to be close together, because some day, before very long, it's already started, there is a neighborliness that has proved to be profitable. And that is the students taking work at Idaho,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: wrapped up, or taken over by the Southern Idaho people. We fought that from the beginning of the university, to the present time, there has always been an inclination for the Southern Idaho people to think that they should have, have a part of the university, or all of the university down there. And one thing I have to tell you, that it all seemed humorous to me, and they worked so hard for it. They finally settled, they got the capital from Boy, from Lewiston down to Boise, and in the beginning. And then, when the university came, they, they, they, then unseth in getting the university movements of the night. But they finally agreed that they would settle for the state penitentiary. But like my brother said, the, the what they forgot, those people down in Boise, around the, in that southern part of the state, what they forgot was that the penitentiary didn't have an alumni association like the state of Idaho has. And so that's why I'm an alum of Idaho, and I've done all I can for the city of Moscow, for the University of Idaho. And if all the young, young graduates, thousands of them now, in my class, I think our student body was somewhere around 500, and I think we had maybe less than 200. Maybe I could say 100, but I think less than 200 graduates in 1913, 1912. I got to one year before I was supposed to because I worked so hard. I took 21 credits a semester, and I made it, I took 12 credits when I was in high school. And, and so by the time I got my diploma from high school, I had, had 12 credits in the University of Idaho.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know how they picked the site for the University?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah. I don't know that. That is what I can say there is, it had to be agreed. But all these young businessmen of Moscow, including these people I mentioned, and some of the early professors, which was all, Preston Galt was the first Preston, and Preston McLean was Preston when I was there. And he was in college, and he had been here several years and left the year after I graduated. And he, and my, my mother remembered that there were four, four homesteaders that took up homesteads and the corners of their homesteads met were, came together. In downtown Moscow. There was the McGregor edition, which is southwest, and that's our theater. And southeast, I mean southeast, and southwest was Deacon. Deacon. Now I can't remember if I think it's John Deacon, but it's Deacon. No, it wasn't John. But Deacon. And then the northwest, northeast was Russell. And the northwest was Llewelyn. One of the Llewelyn's. I think there was a Llewelyn that took that homestead out there, but he didn't even keep that railroad. But I think one of the Llewelyn's took this. I should ask, Mrs. Nonini, she knows that she was Llewelyn. Her mother was Llewelyn. I'll ask her someday about that, because that's one point I want to know. And I want to know the early history of Moscow and the early history of the universities are two things that I want to learn more about before my days are ended.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And what did you ask?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: About how they picked the site. Oh, yeah. And so then at first they wanted to make the university. They were, oh, this Mr. Brigham and these people worked so hard. And of course, Spokane was anxious to have R .I. the whole state university in Moscow, because that was closer to Spokane. And Spokane Falls at that time was beginning to be quite a city in Spokane Falls. And so they sent representatives down here to help these men decide. And they had this Mr. Brigham or some young fellow down in the legislature. He was a member of the first legislature. And he was one of the earliest legislators, was one of the fellows that worked so hard for the university. And I'll tell you where you can get that story, and I'll be glad to loan you that, is in Ray Kidd's history of the university. See, and I'll loan you that book if I'm sure I can get it back, but I'm sure I couldn't get another one. And then, so Deaton, they decided that it was kind of far from the railroad. The new railroads were coming into Moscow. The Northern Stiffic had been, I just completed, I think, and they wanted them to come in. And they decided it was kind of far, if they built the university out in the northwest part of town, it was kind of far from the railroad depots. And the students would have to come by rail from southern Idaho. And there were no other way, no cars. There were very few cars. And they'd come up on that awful, awful terrible, terrible highway with just a dirt road, you might say, in lots of places, from southern Idaho. So they knew that they had to compromise on that point, and not build the university here, but over on the west side, near the depots. So Mr. Deacon, the homesteader there, who owned his home, was where the Forest Laboratory is. That was the old Deacon home there. And I don't remember his loan cabin, but there was one. I remember it when the new home was built there. And the Shannon's owned it later. Shannon's. And he sold 20 acres to the campus for $4 ,000. That was what he charged. And there was no timber. And there was never timber, as far as most people say. There was never timber right around where Moscow is right now, or where in the valley here. And this was the valley most likely created by upheavals when the mountains, Rocky Mountains were created. And this left a valley filled with dirt. So if there was timber here in this Palouse Valley, at any time, it's been ages, centuries, millions of years ago maybe. And when it was filled with overflow of lava or sand and rocks and stuff, and it became eroded. So that's the reason that the soil around Moscow is rich as it is. Because there was lots of vegetation, and there was rocks, erosion made the soil here, and made this rich soil. And that extends clear out to the toy and out all along the peak of this Paradise Valley. And way up, as far as Troy and farther, to even up to Beaubill and that is good soil. But not as good as it is right around here. And Whitman County is included. Clear down to all the ridges around here, you know. They call them, what they call them, my dad used to call them. He used to thrash down, and he even took the thrash machine down and ferried it across the river and thrashed in the early days up at Tammany and around Lewiston. And took his whole thrashing up it. And that's why he was, if he'd tried to make a living on that 240 acres, that's one Newton, or John, dealman, had most likely taken up a whole 160 acres. And his brother, another 160 acres, which made 320, but our farm is only 240 acres, which most likely means that we don't know that for sure. He sold part of his, he sold 80 acres to that 160 that the other brother did. And that would maybe be that scrubby land of where Paradise Mountains were the east end of Paradise, or up to the Paradise, over the Paradise. And so he sold that. There were other people that came later, like a man we called Chip Johnson, who's Norwegian. He had a little acreage on top of the Paradise Mountain, and it's still there, but I don't know who lives there. He's been dead years, for good years. He made the little, oh, he made the little water trough that we used for the horses, and made it out of the wood that he got off his farm up there, and they had a big family. They'd walk from that, tip of that Paradise Mountain, walk down and walk over our land. My dad always let him do that. And they'd nearly always stop and get a drink of water, but mother usually had something to get him to eat, like coffee and rolls, and maybe a feed. And then they'd walk to town and walk back, all in one day. They'd shop, he'd have a gunny sack, and she would have a big bag that she'd made out of old material, I suppose. And they'd buy their groceries and carry them on their back, home to their family. Because what I remember when I wasn't very big was that she was going to have another baby, and not very far away either.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And she was going, and they came, and she walked to town, and my goodness, oh my goodness, is that on?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I didn't remember that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why didn't you tell me? What's her name?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And she had her baby, I think, the next day. And she'd walk to town and walk back. And he made this tub, we called it, a cup. Some of them called it a cup, for the watering tub. He built our first barn. He was a carpenter. And he rolled it down from Paradise Mountain, rolled it down over our farm, our whole farm. See there, look at that picture, and you can tell, get an idea about the hills, and that he rolled that over and down. And they had it, and so I had to put a picture of that there. I only had one picture. And we used it many years, by the old barn. And of course, I don't know where it's been destroyed since then.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And I, now what shall I tell you? Do you think that's enough for the university?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: In fact, I think you can get more information through that book than in the history of the university. But they bought it for 20, and they had popular trees around the whole 20 acres. And they built the one building, you know, that's in here. The one building. And I remember, I don't remember when they built it, but I remember when it burned. And I remember going with Mother, walking. When I was four years old, walking to a concert at the university, because Mother was interested in music, and one of the girls that was giving this concert, a student in music, they had a music school, the very first thing. Because it was always a cultural town, really, besides being a prime town. And she was having, it was this Eva Sweets concert. And she was one of the girls that Mother used to go and babysit. See, she'd go between the two families, the Sweets family and this Hannah family. And so I walked with Mother. She wouldn't leave me alone, you know, at home. And the other kids were big enough to be out with Dad off on the farm. But, well, no, let's see.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did the administration building burn that day, that you were walking to the concert?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: No, no, no. No, no, I don't know.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I was, gee, I was, goodness, I was born in 1890, and this was 1906, that the university burned. So I was 16 years old then, I remember that. I think I was in high school, or eighth grade, or something like that. High school or eighth grade. When that burned. But I remember my father coming to wake me, because he always used to, it took us an hour to walk to school, and I had to get lunches ready for seven brothers and two sisters. And now two sisters, because the youngest sister was born when I graduated from the university. But there were six, about six of the brothers and sisters that I had to get lunch for and help them get ready for school and look nice. And that's what my brother says to this brother here. He's the one who writes to me all. He says, oh, he said, I'll never forget how you just, we had to have our hair combed. We had to have a pre -insured. We had to have a tie -tied. And everything, every morning before we went to school, he says, how I remember. That little book there. Look at the front page of that. I'm going to take a little minute, and I'll find this picture that I'm going to show you.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I can't find it. See, that, and a big screw. And they had the cooking tent and tents for them to sleep in. And he had to have one. They could only work in the summertime. They couldn't work in the wintertime. And they had severe winters those days, years. And so he rented this north farm. He didn't rent the land. But he rented the buildings on that farm. And there were two great big barns. Great big barns. And those people had come from the east, and they saw all this grass, and they were going to raise cattle and get real rich. And they didn't have anything. A walking plow. No way to cultivate this land. It was all in virgin soil. And so they couldn't even make a living. No railroads, no transportation, no way in the world to make it. They had to live on what they could raise. And then the winters were real severe. And I told that in a story. But this Mrs. McGuire, who lived on the farm next to the Northrop Place west, was one of the log cabin. That's one log cabin that I remember well. And then this cabin on the Northrop Place. And the cabin on our place, on our farm, was a little log cabin. And we used it for the blacksmith shop. But I think I'm the only one that might be my oldest brother to see. He passed away in 54. That he remembered that log cabin. But it was used for our blacksmith shop. So there was those three cabins. And then there was the Taylor cabin. And I guess I remember maybe more than that. And there was one Stuart cabin that's a little bit farther west.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you going to say about Mrs.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: McGuire?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, Mrs. McGuire told this story.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: She lived on there, Homestead, McGuire, Homestead. And my brother owns that now. And dad and his brother bought our land for $6 an acre. And now that man, my brother, is part of that McGuire land that he bought besides the home place. And he had the bar place and the McGuire place. And he bought it 15, 20 years ago. And paid a lot for it. But now part of that McGuire place has been taken in where that tavern is and that out there. And east of that tavern is my brother's farm. And he's selling, sold some of it for commercial for $10 ,000 for two acres, you know. And maybe he can get $10 ,000 an acre for some of it that's been divided into lots. It's been platted and the water's on it. And there's electricity's up there. So it's all ready for sale. And he'll sell it. He'll make as much on 35 acres, which he's holding out. Maybe as they paid for all that land. And now what I've been praying for is that my niece's husband is a very good boy and a very active man. And he's been farming for three or four years since my brother had his heart attack. He's gotten over it, though, thank goodness. But he isn't going to farm anymore. And so that farm, home farm, is going to be there in all the other land, too. Except that 35 acres that he's held out. And he can make as much on that 35 acres as we used to make on all the land, you know. But that's what...

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Are you living on the farm?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No, we're just renting a house.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: That's on the farm.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So what's the story about Mrs. McGuire?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh, yeah, you still didn't get that. All right.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Mrs. McGuire, she was one of the homesteaders, see. That was the third farm over. And Northridge, I think they were related some way. But we don't know that. I don't know that. And maybe Mother did. I don't know. And she said that one of the first winters they came here, one of the first none, I think, and they had a lot of cattle. And they'd done like the Northridge in there. They built a big barn and had cattle and was going to get...make a good living there. And they'd been people that hadn't even been farmers back east, came over in wagons in 1871, I think. And so cattle just play died, you know. They just died. And they just couldn't save them. And then they...but they managed to keep one because they didn't have any grass. It was too cold. It froze. And they, I guess, they didn't haul the hay in. They just...I don't know about that. But anyhow, she said when she came over, when I was born, she came over to help. That's what they used to do, you know. They did have a doctor, though. My mother had a doctor. But all of our 10 children were born on the farm in that bed there instead of in hospital. And Miss McGuire said, we saved one cow. But we finally, before the very last, she said we had to empty the straw out of all our mattresses. And the way to keep that cow living, and they didn't. They had three cows, but the others died, but one cow survived. And they emptied the mattresses, the straw hair, whatever they called it. It was dry hay, you know, in the mattresses. And she survived on that. And they managed to have some milk, you know. And oh, it was tough. It was really rough.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: She said, well, she said, you think you've got it bad now?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And she told my mother. But then by that time, they'd already even got the railroad in here. By the time I was born. And things were getting a lot better.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you have any idea, you know, of why the people from Homestead, a lot of them didn't stay around?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: That's the reason they couldn't make a living. There wasn't hardly any of them that did. Look at the nice, the ... that had our place. I'm going to show you what I'm doing. Is, I printed this in a rough manner. I printed the better one.
